Safeguard
We had the detention reviewed within hours.
We got the order quashed the same day.
The judge had the custodian produce the prisoner.
I will get my client released tonight.
Counsel, you have filed a petition for habeas corpus. Tell the court why this detention is unlawful.
My Lord, my client has been held for six days. No warrant, no charge, no hearing. The detention is plainly arbitrary.
He has been held incommunicado, with no access to counsel. I ask the court to issue the writ and have the custodian produce him before this bench today.
The State says it is investigating. Is that not a lawful basis to hold the detainee?
No, My Lord. Investigation is not detention. Liberty cannot be removed without a lawful order. If they have grounds, let them charge him; if not, we must get him released tonight.
And any statement taken in those six days was the fruit of coercion. I ask the court to have it excluded and to recognise the writ as the citizen's first safeguard.
The writ is granted. The State must justify the detention by noon or have the detainee released.

Habeas corpus is the oldest safeguard a citizen has, and the fastest weapon in your hands as a defender. It does not wait for a trial. The words mean: produce the body. When you file it, you force the State to bring your client before a judge and to justify the detention on the spot, or release the person at once. That is the whole power of the writ. So do not argue guilt or innocence here; argue liberty. Liberty cannot be removed without a lawful order, and no one may be held arbitrarily, with no warrant, no charge and no hearing. If your client is held incommunicado, you have him produced; if the State cannot justify it, you get him released. Move quickly, because every hour of an unlawful detention is an hour of injustice.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. The High Court today granted a petition for habeas corpus in the case of the detainee Andrade. The petitioner had argued that the detention was arbitrary and unlawful: the client had been held for six days with no warrant, no charge and no hearing, and kept incommunicado without access to a defender. The State asked the court to accept that an ongoing investigation was a sufficient basis to hold him. The court disagreed, recognising the writ as the citizen's first safeguard. It ordered the State to justify the detention by noon or have the detainee released, and directed that the custodian produce him before the bench. We understand the client was freed the same afternoon.
